mapping.a_element: radio-frequency spectroscopy to probe excitation spectra in a Fermi gas
mapping.b_element: depletion spectroscopy combined with resonant-enhanced multi-photon ionization to determine molecular states

mapping.a_element: spatially resolved spectral response obtained via in situ phase-contrast imaging and 3D reconstruction
mapping.b_element: time-of-flight spectroscopy to resolve molecular states

mapping.a_element: asymmetric excitation line shape with a peak at 0.48(4)ε_F
mapping.b_element: selective production of specific rovibrational states (e.g., v"=0, J"=0 or J"=2)

brief_justification: While both papers use spectroscopic techniques to probe quantum states, paper A focuses on a many-body Fermi gas with resonant interactions and a spectral gap driven by collective physics, whereas paper B employs photo-association and depletion spectroscopy to selectively populate and detect discrete molecular rovibrational states. The underlying mechanisms (many-body physics vs. molecular spectroscopy) and governing equations differ fundamentally.
